section.title: Clean and Unclean Animals
section.sectionRationale: The book now turns from the sacrificial and ordination narratives to the purity regulations, beginning with dietary law. This chapter fulfills the charge just given to Aaron in chapter ten, to teach Israel the difference between clean and unclean, grounding the entire system in God's own holiness.
section.thesis: God gives Israel detailed food laws distinguishing clean from unclean animals on land, in water, and in the air, grounding the whole system in His own repeated command, be holy, because I am holy, so that a set apart diet would form a people visibly set apart to the LORD.
section.frameNote: Four movements: clean and unclean land animals; clean and unclean water creatures and birds; unclean swarming and crawling things and the defilement they cause; and the closing summons to holiness that grounds the whole chapter.

units.label: Land animals
units.note: split hoof and chewing the cud

verses.text: The LORD spoke again to Moses and Aaron, telling them,
“Say to the Israelites, ‘Of all the beasts of the earth, these ones you may eat:
You may eat any animal that has a split hoof completely divided and that chews the cud.
But of those that only chew the cud or only have a divided hoof, you are not to eat the following: The camel, though it chews the cud, does not have a divided hoof; it is {{tame|unclean}} for you.
The rock badger, though it chews the cud, does not have a divided hoof; it is unclean for you.
The rabbit, though it chews the cud, does not have a divided hoof; it is unclean for you.
And the pig, though it has a split hoof completely divided, does not chew the cud; it is unclean for you.
You must not eat their meat or touch their carcasses; they are unclean for you.
Of all the creatures that live in the water, whether in the seas or in the streams, you may eat anything with fins and scales.
But the following among all the teeming life and creatures in the water are detestable to you: everything in the seas or streams that does not have fins and scales.
They shall be an abomination to you; you must not eat their meat, and you must detest their carcasses.
Everything in the water that does not have fins and scales shall be detestable to you.
Additionally, you are to detest the following birds, and they must not be eaten because they are detestable: the eagle, the bearded vulture, the black vulture,
the kite, any kind of falcon,
any kind of raven,
the ostrich, the screech owl, the gull, any kind of hawk,
the little owl, the cormorant, the great owl,
the white owl, the desert owl, the osprey,
the stork, any kind of heron, the hoopoe, and the bat.
All flying insects that walk on all fours are detestable to you.
However, you may eat the following kinds of flying insects that walk on all fours: those having jointed legs above their feet for hopping on the ground.
Of these you may eat any kind of locust, katydid, cricket, or grasshopper.
All other flying insects that have four legs are detestable to you.
These creatures will make you unclean. Whoever touches their carcasses will be unclean until evening,
and whoever picks up one of their carcasses must wash his clothes, and he will be unclean until evening.
Every animal with hooves not completely divided or that does not chew the cud is unclean for you. Whoever touches any of them will be unclean.
All the four-footed animals that walk on their paws are unclean for you; whoever touches their carcasses will be unclean until evening,
and anyone who picks up a carcass must wash his clothes, and he will be unclean until evening. They are unclean for you.
The following creatures that move along the ground are unclean for you: the mole, the mouse, any kind of great lizard,
the gecko, the monitor lizard, the common lizard, the skink, and the chameleon.
These animals are unclean for you among all the crawling creatures. Whoever touches them when they are dead shall be unclean until evening.

When one of them dies and falls on something, that article becomes unclean; any article of wood, clothing, leather, sackcloth, or any implement used for work must be rinsed with water and will remain unclean until evening; then it will be clean.
If any of them falls into a clay pot, everything in it will be unclean; you must break the pot.
Any food coming into contact with water from that pot will be unclean, and any drink in such a container will be unclean.
Anything upon which one of their carcasses falls will be unclean. If it is an oven or cooking pot, it must be smashed; it is unclean and will remain unclean for you.
Nevertheless, a spring or cistern containing water will remain clean, but one who touches a carcass in it will be unclean.
If a carcass falls on any seed for sowing, the seed is clean;
but if water has been put on the seed and a carcass falls on it, it is unclean for you.
If an animal that you may eat dies, anyone who touches the carcass will be unclean until evening.
Whoever eats from the carcass must wash his clothes and will be unclean until evening, and anyone who picks up the carcass must wash his clothes and will be unclean until evening.

units.label: Be holy, because I am holy
units.note: the ground for the whole chapter

verses.text: Every creature that moves along the ground is detestable; it must not be eaten.
Do not eat any creature that moves along the ground, whether it crawls on its belly or walks on four or more feet; for such creatures are detestable.
Do not defile yourselves by any crawling creature; do not become unclean or defiled by them.
For I am the LORD your God; consecrate yourselves, therefore, and be {{qadosh|holy}}, because I am holy. You must not defile yourselves by any creature that crawls along the ground.
For I am the LORD, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt so that I would be your God; therefore be holy, because I am holy.
This is the law regarding animals, birds, all living creatures that move in the water, and all creatures that crawl along the ground.
You must distinguish between the unclean and the clean, between animals that may be eaten and those that may not.’”

tame.gloss: Unclean, ceremonially unfit for approach to God or His sanctuary. Not a statement about hygiene or moral evil in itself, but a category marking certain creatures as outside the boundary of what a holy people may eat or touch without further ritual consequence.

qadosh.gloss: Holy, set apart. The chapter's repeated refrain, be holy, because I am holy, grounds every dietary distinction in God's own character; Israel's diet was meant to visibly embody their identity as a people set apart to Him.

basic.context: This chapter fulfills the charge given to Aaron at the end of chapter ten, to teach Israel the difference between clean and unclean. It works through land animals, water creatures, birds, and swarming things, listing what may and may not be eaten, and describing how contact with unclean carcasses could defile a person or object until evening. The chapter closes by grounding the entire system in God's own holiness: be holy, because I am holy.
basic.hermeneutics: This gives Israel's actual old covenant dietary law; the New Testament explicitly declares these food laws fulfilled and no longer binding, Mark 7:19, Acts 10:15, Acts 15. Its abiding truth is not the specific animals but the principle grounding the whole chapter, that God's people are called to visible, distinctive holiness because He Himself is holy.
basic.originalLanguages: Tame, unclean, does not necessarily mean sinful or dirty in a moral sense, but marks certain creatures as outside the boundary of what this holy people could eat without becoming ceremonially unfit for God's presence. The chapter's driving logic is qadosh, holy, repeated four times in the closing verses, tying Israel's entire diet to God's own character as the reason behind it.
basic.typology: The sheet of unclean animals shown to Peter in Acts 10, alongside God's declaration what God has made clean, do not call unclean, anticipates the very reversal this chapter's laws would undergo once the gospel went to the nations. The careful boundary between clean and unclean itself pictures the deeper boundary between sin and holiness that only Christ's blood, not diet, can finally cross.
basic.christ: Mark 7:19 records Jesus declaring all foods clean, and Acts 10 shows this truth applied directly to Peter's vision of clean and unclean animals, teaching that the gospel breaks down the very boundary these food laws once maintained between Jew and Gentile, Ephesians 2:14-15. What actually defiles a person, Jesus taught, comes from the heart, not from what is eaten, Mark 7:20-23, a truth only fully remedied by Christ's cleansing work.
basic.god: God cares about the whole of ordinary life, down to what His people eat, showing that holiness was never meant to be confined to formal worship alone. His repeated ground for these laws, I am holy, reveals a God who forms His people's identity, even their diet, around who He is, not around arbitrary preference.
basic.crossReferences: Mark 7:19 records Jesus declaring all foods clean. Acts 10:9-16 shows Peter's vision of the sheet with unclean animals and God's declaration that what He has made clean should not be called unclean. Acts 15 records the Jerusalem council settling that Gentile believers need not keep these food laws.
basic.oneStory: This chapter forms Israel into a visibly distinct people through their diet, grounding every distinction in God's own holiness, be holy, because I am holy. The New Testament shows this entire system fulfilled in Christ, whose finished work makes people holy from the heart, tearing down the dietary wall that once separated Jew from Gentile.

context.literary: This chapter immediately follows the charge given to Aaron in chapter 10:10-11 to distinguish holy from common and clean from unclean and to teach these distinctions to Israel; chapter 11 is that teaching put into detailed practice.
context.historical: Surrounding ancient Near Eastern cultures had their own food taboos, but Israel's laws were distinctive in being grounded explicitly and repeatedly in the character of their God rather than in magic, taboo, or hygiene theory alone.
context.canonical: This chapter's food laws become one of the clearest points at which the New Testament announces the fulfillment and setting aside of old covenant ceremonial law, discussed directly by Jesus, Mark 7, and applied to the early church's mission to the Gentiles in Acts 10 and 15.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To give Israel detailed dietary regulations distinguishing clean from unclean creatures, grounding the entire system explicitly in God's own holiness, so that Israel's daily life would visibly embody their calling as a people set apart to Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This prescribed actual dietary law for old covenant Israel; the New Testament explicitly and repeatedly declares it fulfilled and no longer binding on believers, Mark 7:19, Acts 10, Acts 15. What abides is the underlying principle, that God's people are called to visible holiness because He is holy, though the specific means of expressing that holiness has changed under the new covenant.

debates.question: Why were these particular animals designated unclean rather than others?

views.label: Symbolic association with death, violence, or disorder
views.case: Many scholars note that many forbidden animals are predators, scavengers, or creatures associated with carcasses and death, fitting patterns that would symbolically clash with a people called to represent life and order under a holy God.

views.label: A test of obedience without a fully explained rationale
views.case: Others argue Scripture itself never explains the specific logic behind each category, and caution against inventing a system the text does not state explicitly, viewing the primary point simply as visible obedience and distinctiveness, whatever the underlying reasoning.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the laws served to mark Israel as visibly distinct and obedient to God, whether or not the precise logic behind each specific animal can be fully recovered.

study.originalLanguages: The word tame, unclean, marks a ritual and dietary category rather than necessarily a moral or hygienic one, placing certain creatures outside the boundary of what this set apart people could eat. The chapter's repeated ground, qadosh, holy, appearing four times in verses 44 to 45, ties this entire detailed system directly to God's own character as the reason Israel was to obey it.

typology.type: The boundary between clean and unclean foods
typology.fulfillment: The breaking down of the wall between Jew and Gentile in Christ, Ephesians 2:14-15, and the declaration that all foods are clean, Mark 7:19.

typology.type: Defilement by contact, spreading from carcass to vessel to person
typology.fulfillment: The deeper truth that defilement actually flows from the human heart, addressed only by the cleansing Christ provides, Mark 7:20-23.

study.christ: Jesus directly addresses this chapter's concerns in Mark 7:14-23, teaching that nothing entering a person from outside can defile them, since defilement comes from the heart, and Mark notes this declaration made all foods clean. Peter's vision in Acts 10:9-16, where God declares clean animals this chapter had called unclean, becomes the turning point that opens the gospel fully to Gentiles, since Christ's finished work removes the very boundary these food laws once marked out.
study.god: God's concern for holiness reaches into the most ordinary details of daily life, what His people eat and touch, showing that no part of life sits outside His claim. The chapter's repeated refrain, I am holy, reveals a God who forms His people's whole identity, even something as basic as diet, around who He is.

crossReferences.note: Jesus teaches that defilement comes from the heart, not from food, and Mark notes this declaration made all foods clean.
Peter's vision of the sheet with unclean animals, and God's declaration that what He has made clean should not be called unclean.
Christ has broken down the dividing wall of hostility, abolishing the law's regulations that separated Jew from Gentile.

study.oneStory: This chapter set Israel visibly apart through their diet, grounding the whole system in God's own holiness, be holy, because I am holy. The New Testament shows this entire system fulfilled and set aside in Christ, whose cleansing work addresses the deeper defilement of the human heart and whose gospel breaks down the very wall these food laws once maintained between Jew and Gentile.

quiz.q: What two characteristics must a land animal have for Israel to eat it?

options.text: A completely divided hoof and chewing the cud.
options.note: Verse 3 gives exactly these two required characteristics.

options.text: Living only in dry desert regions.
options.note: Habitat is not the criterion given; the text specifies hoof and cud characteristics.

options.text: Being domesticated rather than wild.
options.note: Domestication is not the stated criterion; several forbidden animals, like the pig, could be domesticated, while some permitted animals were wild.

options.text: No criteria are given.
options.note: Verse 3 gives explicit, specific criteria.

quiz.q: What characteristic makes a water creature clean and permissible to eat?

options.text: Having fins and scales.
options.note: Verse 9 explicitly gives this requirement for water creatures.

options.text: Living exclusively in fresh water rivers.
options.note: The text specifies both seas and streams, and the criterion given is fins and scales, not the type of water.

options.text: Being caught only by net, never by hook.
options.note: The method of catching is not addressed; the criterion is physical, fins and scales.

options.text: No criterion is given.
options.note: Verse 9 gives an explicit criterion.

quiz.q: What happens to a clay pot if an unclean crawling creature falls into it?

options.text: It must be broken, since everything in it becomes unclean.
options.note: Verse 33 explicitly requires breaking the clay pot in this situation.

options.text: It only needs to be rinsed with water.
options.note: Rinsing is the remedy for wood, clothing, leather, or sackcloth, verse 32; a clay pot specifically must be broken, verse 33.

options.text: Nothing needs to be done; clay pots are unaffected.
options.note: Verse 33 explicitly states the pot becomes unclean and must be broken.

options.text: The pot must be sold to a foreigner.
options.note: No such instruction is given; the pot is simply broken.

quiz.q: What reason does God give repeatedly at the close of this chapter for Israel to observe these laws?

options.text: Because I am holy, so you must be holy.
options.note: Verses 44 and 45 both repeat this exact grounding for the entire chapter's laws.

options.text: Because these animals carry disease.
options.note: The text grounds the laws explicitly in God's holiness and Israel's calling, not in a stated medical or hygienic rationale.

options.text: Because neighboring nations demanded it.
options.note: The stated reason is God's own holiness and His deliverance of Israel from Egypt, not the demands of other nations.

options.text: No reason is given.
options.note: Verses 44 to 45 explicitly and repeatedly give the reason.

quiz.q: According to Mark 7:19 and Acts 10, how does the New Testament treat these Old Testament food laws?

options.text: Jesus declares all foods clean, and Peter's vision confirms that what God has made clean should not be called unclean.
options.note: Mark 7:19 and Acts 10:9-16 both directly address and set aside this chapter's dietary distinctions.

options.text: The New Testament reaffirms these laws as permanently binding on all believers.
options.note: Mark 7 and Acts 10 explicitly move in the opposite direction, declaring these distinctions fulfilled and set aside.

options.text: The New Testament never mentions food laws at all.
options.note: Mark 7, Acts 10, and Acts 15 all address this topic directly and substantially.

options.text: No connection exists between this chapter and the New Testament.
options.note: Mark 7 and Acts 10 draw a direct and explicit connection to this very chapter's categories.

questions: Israel's diet itself marked them as a people set apart to God, visible even in ordinary meals. What ordinary, everyday part of your life could visibly reflect that you belong to God, even though no one is watching closely?
The reason given four times in this chapter is simply, I am holy. How much of your own obedience to God is rooted in who He is, rather than in what benefits you personally?

Jesus declared all foods clean, teaching that true defilement comes from the heart, not from what enters the body. What is actually coming out of your heart lately, in your words and actions, that reveals what needs to change?
Peter had to learn, through a vision, not to call unclean what God had made clean. Where might you still be drawing lines between people or things that Christ has already broken down?

This entire system of clean and unclean anticipated a deeper cleansing only Christ could provide. Do you still try to make yourself acceptable to God through external behavior, or do you rest in the cleansing He has already accomplished?

translationNotes.note: The prohibition on pork, though it has a split hoof, does not chew the cud, is one of the best known food laws in this chapter and became a significant marker of Jewish identity throughout later history.

Flying insects that walk on all fours likely describes insects like locusts, whose hind legs for jumping were distinguished from their other walking legs, explaining the permitted exception in verses 21 to 22.

Consecrate yourselves translates a Hebrew reflexive form of qadash, indicating an active response Israel must make to God's holiness, not merely a passive status; the grounding for the command is repeated twice, verses 44 and 45, for emphasis.
